responses.response: The counter to my own S1 argument is implicit in A3: if non-state maintainers remain outside uniform equity rules, advantaged families simply relocate to church and foundation schools, and sorting is relocated rather than reduced — meaning the autonomy I defend becomes the very channel that defeats the reform's purpose. I accept the factual force of this: an exemption without any accountability creates exactly the displacement risk A3 and A7 describe. But the answer is not to erase maintainer autonomy — it is to distinguish autonomy over mission and pedagogy from opacity about outcomes. We can accept transparency and outcome-reporting obligations (published admissions criteria, SES-composition disclosure) without accepting uniform quota mandates that override mission-based admissions. Subsidiarity has never meant unaccountability; it means the state does not dictate our internal criteria, not that our aggregate effects escape public scrutiny.
responses.new_condition: We would support S1 if it applies binding transparency and SES-composition reporting requirements to church and foundation schools (public disclosure, not admissions quotas), with a review mechanism that assesses whether voluntary or lightly-regulated cross-maintainer measures actually prevent the displacement A3 predicts; only if that fails within an agreed period would we accept discussion of stronger cross-maintainer mandates.

responses.response: A9 is my own argument on S2; the strongest counter to it comes from A7 and A10, which show that the scenario's own equity rationale (peer-composition benefit) is weak/contested, and that non-state absorption of displaced demand is the dominant, near-certain effect rather than a side risk. This sharpens rather than defeats my position: if non-state absorption is the main mechanism by which S2 'works' at all, then treating our expansion as sabotage is doubly unjustified — we are not undermining a robust equity mechanism, we are substituting for a policy that has no credible equity mechanism of its own. That said, A7's point that this relocates sorting rather than reducing it is a genuine problem for the public good frame I invoke (plurality), because uncontrolled non-state expansion under an equity banner does deepen stratification in aggregate, even if not by our design. I cannot deny that outcome by asserting institutional survival alone.
responses.new_condition: We would support S2 only if, alongside per-pupil demographic-contraction funding for non-state maintainers, the reform also requires equivalent transparency/SES-reporting from non-state selective intake during the phase-down — so that whatever expansion occurs to preserve our viability is at least visible and measurable against the equity goal, rather than a silent, unaccountable relocation of sorting.

responses.response: A12 concedes the empirical uncertainty I raised in my S3 opposition — that sorting-migration to non-state channels is undocumented in the Hungarian context and reversal risk is real but unquantified — but it reframes this as a reason to still pursue S3 with better lock-in and anti-stratification levers, rather than as decisive grounds to withdraw the reform. That is a fair challenge to my position: if the uncertainty cuts both ways (we don't know sorting will migrate to us, just as we don't know compensation will work), then my opposition to S3 rests partly on an unproven fear symmetric to S4's unproven hope. I maintain that the burden should fall on those proposing structural abolition, not on those defending an existing plural arrangement, but I recognise this is a values-based allocation of the burden of proof, not a factual rebuttal — A12's proponents would reasonably allocate it the other way given the documented severity of the SES gradient at age 10.
responses.new_condition: unchanged from prior response — explicit regulatory protection against non-state schools being blamed for migration they did not cause, plus front-loaded investment and multi-cycle political lock-in, remain the conditions that would move us from opposition to conditional support.
